Executive Briefing 25 Mar 2026
Opening
The biggest move this week is New Mexico's finalized PFAS labeling rules landing March 23 — actionable now, not just proposed. The fluoropolymer exemption question in NM remains the higher-stakes watch item given direct product implications.
Key Developments
• New Mexico PFAS labeling rules finalized March 23: Products with intentionally added PFAS sold in NM now face a concrete labeling obligation — assess whether any finished goods or components sold into NM trigger this, and flag it for the Assent data collection workflow since supplier declarations will underpin any labeling determination.<br>• NM Legislature's Joint Memorial on fluoropolymer exemption is the real threat: NMED has been directed to evaluate whether to retain the fluoropolymer carve-out — if that exemption falls, NM becomes a direct product compliance problem on top of MN PRISM, and the reformulation prioritization calculus changes materially.<br>• DEHP 60-day notice filed March 24 against phone cases at Best Buy (AG# 2026-01348): Enforcement wave continues against consumer-facing plastic/vinyl products; if the company sources or sells any accessory-adjacent components in CA retail channels, confirm DEHP levels in any PVC-containing parts with suppliers now.<br>• New Mexico labeling and exemption developments together represent a second state-level PFAS product compliance front opening in parallel with MN — the supplier education campaign messaging should be updated to reference NM obligations alongside PRISM, particularly for distributors selling into both states.
Outlook
Watch NMED's timeline for the fluoropolymer exemption evaluation report — if they signal a fast-track review, it accelerates the reformulation priority list. Also monitor whether Wisconsin's PFAS bill package advances to product-specific restrictions, which would add a third Midwest state to the compliance map.
New Mexico's PFAS Protection Act is one of the few state laws that explicitly directed a regulatory agency to evaluate its own exemptions on a fixed legislative timeline — most exemptions quietly persist until litigation forces a review. The legislature essentially built a sunset review into the statute by design.

Article Status

Faded row = older than 30 days

High Deadline <90 days or active enforcement
Medium Proposed rule or 90–365 days out
Low Long-range or informational
Company Relevance
(no badge)  Direct impact on our products/supply chain
Indirect Worth knowing — signals trends for us (hover for why)
Monitor General news — no foreseeable company impact
Impact Score (1–10)
7+ High company impact
4–6 Moderate company impact
1–3 Low company impact
Score weights: product risk (35%), supply chain (25%), financial (20%), timeline (20%)

PFAS 1 new
13
updates
New Mexico Enacts Sweeping PFAS Product Labeling Obligations
▼
Open PFAS Dashboard ↗
New New Mexico Enacts Sweeping PFAS Product Labeling Obligations MEDIUM 6.2
JD Supra PFAS
New Mexico has enacted comprehensive PFAS labeling requirements for consumer products containing intentionally added PFAS, requiring manufacturers to disclose PFAS presence on product labels. The law builds on the state's PFAS Protection Act and follows NMED's finalized reporting and labeling rule. Fluoropolymer exemption status remains under legislative review via a joint memorial directing NMED to report on whether the exemption should be retained.
New Mexico Finalizes PFAS Product Labeling Rule for Consumer Products MEDIUM 6.5
New Mexico Environment Dept · 2026-03-23
New Mexico NMED approved and finalized rules requiring labeling of PFAS in consumer products sold in the state, following a rulemaking process initiated in October 2025. The rule mandates that manufacturers disclose intentionally added PFAS on product labels prior to sale in New Mexico. A separate legislative joint memorial calls on NMED to evaluate whether fluoropolymer exemptions should be retained, which is directly relevant to companies using fluoropolymer coatings.
New Mexico Legislature Reviews Fluoropolymer Exemption in PFAS Act MEDIUM 6.8
Bergeson & Campbell · 2026-02-23
The New Mexico Legislature passed a joint memorial directing NMED to evaluate whether the fluoropolymer exemption in the state's PFAS Protection Act should be retained. This signals legislative pressure to potentially eliminate exemptions that currently protect fluoropolymer coating applications from product-level prohibitions and labeling mandates. The NMED report could set a precedent for other states to revisit similar exemptions.
Minnesota PRISM System Updated; MPCA Webinar on CUUs Held in February HIGH 7.7
Bergeson & Campbell · 2026-03-09
Minnesota MPCA updated the PRISM reporting system in early March 2026, and held a February 2026 webinar focused on Commercially Used Unavoidable Uses (CUUs) — a key exemption category for manufacturers who cannot yet reformulate. The system is now live and accepting registrations ahead of the July 1, 2026 deadline. Manufacturers and distributors selling PFAS-containing products in Minnesota must register or lose the ability to legally sell those products in the state.
2026-07-01
Minnesota Publishes Final PFAS in Products Reporting and Fees Rule HIGH 8.7
Bergeson & Campbell · 2025-12-09
Minnesota published its final rule governing PFAS-in-products reporting requirements and associated fee structure, cementing the legal framework for the July 1, 2026 PRISM registration deadline. The rule specifies which product categories require registration, reporting thresholds, and fee obligations for manufacturers selling PFAS-containing products in Minnesota. Non-compliance results in a prohibition on distributor sales of unregistered products in the state.
2026-07-01
MPCA Publishes PRISM Supplemental Guide for Reporting Scenarios HIGH 7.9
MN MPCA
The Minnesota MPCA released a supplemental guide for the PRISM portal to assist manufacturers with various reporting scenarios, including complex product configurations, multi-component assemblies, and CUU claims. This guidance is essential for companies with fluoropolymer-coated components navigating the registration process. The guide also addresses scenarios applicable to distributors whose upstream manufacturers have or have not registered.
2026-07-01
Indirect EPA Adds PFAS to TRI; Federal Register Rule Effective for Reporting Year 2026 MEDIUM 4.9
↗ TRI reporting of PFAS signals EPA escalation of chemical tracking; if we manufacture fluoropolymer coatings in-house or source from suppliers subject to TRI, this expands regulatory oversight of our supply chain.
Federal Register / EPA · 2026-02-27
EPA finalized a Federal Register rule on February 27, 2026, implementing statutory additions of specific PFAS to the Toxics Release Inventory (TRI) under EPCRA, effective for reporting year 2026. This expands facility-level toxic chemical release reporting obligations for manufacturers that use or release listed PFAS above threshold quantities. The EPA also issued a separate proposal to reduce regulatory burden by modifying reporting thresholds for certain PFAS.
2027-07-01
Indirect EPA Proposes Narrowing TSCA Section 8(a)(7) PFAS Reporting; Articles Exempted MEDIUM 3.7
↗ Proposed exemption for imported articles could reduce reporting burden on finished windows/doors, but signals EPA intent to refine PFAS disclosure rules; we monitor for final scope.
Bergeson & Campbell · 2025-11-13
EPA proposed in November 2025 to narrow the scope of the TSCA Section 8(a)(7) PFAS reporting rule, including proposed exemptions for de minimis amounts and imported articles containing PFAS. This could reduce reporting obligations for companies importing PFAS-containing components but also reduces the data available to trace PFAS through supply chains. A companion analysis from Bergeson & Campbell highlights the significant implications for article importers.
Wisconsin Senate Unanimously Passes PFAS Legislation MEDIUM 5.5
Environmental Health News
The Wisconsin Senate passed PFAS legislation unanimously, marking a significant legislative development in a neighboring state to Minnesota with major industrial supply chains. The bills address PFAS contamination accountability and may impose new obligations on manufacturers and distributors operating in Wisconsin. Details on product registration or disclosure requirements are pending review of the final bill text.
Monitor California DTSC Releases Draft Profile for PFAS in Floor Maintenance Products MEDIUM 2.2
Bergeson & Campbell · 2026-03-19
California DTSC released a draft Safer Consumer Products profile for floor maintenance products containing PFAS and held a workshop on March 19, 2026, to receive stakeholder input. If finalized, this profile would require manufacturers and retailers of covered floor maintenance products to conduct alternatives analyses and potentially reformulate. This is a leading indicator of California extending PFAS restrictions into industrial and institutional product categories.
Indirect REACH PFAS Restriction: RAC Adopts Opinion, ECHA Publication Imminent MEDIUM 6.7
↗ EU PFAS restriction under REACH signals potential future US federal alignment and will impact global supply chains for fluoropolymers and coatings; Andersen's export/imported material sourcing may face compliance pressure.
JD Supra PFAS
The EU ECHA Risk Assessment Committee (RAC) adopted its opinion on the proposed universal PFAS restriction under REACH, with ECHA expected to publish the opinion shortly. The restriction, if adopted, would be the broadest chemical restriction in EU history, covering thousands of PFAS substances with limited time-limited derogations for certain industrial uses including fluoropolymers. This has direct implications for EU-facing products and for suppliers of PFAS-containing components with EU operations.
Safer States: Multiple State PFAS Restrictions Now in Effect for 2025 and 2026 MEDIUM 6.4
Safer States
Safer States documented that a broad wave of state PFAS product restrictions and disclosure laws took effect in 2025 and 2026, spanning categories including textiles, food packaging, cookware, firefighting foam, and industrial products. States leading include Minnesota, California, Colorado, Maine, Vermont, and Connecticut, with additional states enacting laws annually. The cumulative multi-state compliance burden is accelerating for manufacturers selling nationally.
Indirect New Jersey AG Reaches $450M PFAS Settlement With 3M LOW 3.7
↗ 3M settlement precedent demonstrates significant financial and reputational liability exposure for PFAS manufacturers/users; signals litigation risk for companies with PFAS products, even if not directly liable.
Legal News
New Jersey AG secured a $450 million settlement with 3M over PFAS contamination liability, adding to the multi-state litigation pressure that drove 3M's exit from the PFAS market. This reinforces that 3M will not re-enter PFAS manufacturing and validates the need for qualified alternative fluoropolymer suppliers. Ongoing state AG litigation against PFAS manufacturers signals continued enforcement escalation across the country.
EPR
1
updates
PSI Webinar Series Prepares Producers for Oregon Packaging EPR Requirements
▼
Open EPR Dashboard ↗
Monitor PSI Webinar Series Prepares Producers for Oregon Packaging EPR Requirements MEDIUM 1.0
Product Stewardship Institute
The Product Stewardship Institute is running a webinar series specifically designed to help producers understand and prepare for Oregon's Packaging EPR program. Oregon's EPR law requires producers shipping packaged products into the state to register with a producer responsibility organization and meet recycled content and recyclability standards. This educational series signals that Oregon's program is moving toward implementation and producers need to act now to assess their obligations.
Oregon PRO registration and compliance timelines under development; monitor ODEQ rulemaking for 2025-2026 dates
REACH
3
updates
ECHA Adds n-Hexane to SVHC Candidate List
▼
Open REACH Dashboard ↗
ECHA Adds n-Hexane to SVHC Candidate List MEDIUM 4.2
ECHA · 2026-02-04
ECHA added n-hexane (CAS 110-54-3) to the REACH SVHC Candidate List on February 4, 2026, based on reproductive toxicity (toxic to reproduction, Category 2). This triggers Article 33 communication obligations for EU article suppliers containing n-hexane above 0.1% w/w, and EU importers must notify ECHA within six months. N-hexane is a common industrial solvent used in adhesives, coatings, cleaning agents, and extraction processes.
2026-08-04 (6-month ECHA notification deadline for EU importers of articles)
Indirect Bisphenol AF (BPAF) Added to SVHC Candidate List MEDIUM 4.1
↗ BPAF is a fluorinated endocrine disruptor; its restriction trajectory signals regulatory momentum against fluorinated compounds that may eventually target our fluoropolymer coatings.
ECHA · 2026-02-04
ECHA added 4,4'-[2,2,2-trifluoro-1-(trifluoromethyl)ethylidene]diphenol and its salts (Bisphenol AF / BPAF) to the SVHC Candidate List on February 4, 2026, identified as an endocrine disruptor affecting human health. BPAF is used as a monomer in the synthesis of certain fluoropolymers, specialty epoxy resins, and polysulfones, making it directly relevant to PFAS-adjacent chemistries. Article 33 obligations apply immediately for EU-supplied articles containing BPAF above 0.1% w/w.
2026-08-04 (6-month ECHA notification deadline for EU importers of articles)
DBDPE Flame Retardant Added to SVHC Candidate List MEDIUM 4.4
ECHA · 2025-11-05
ECHA added 1,1'-(ethane-1,2-diyl)bis[pentabromobenzene] (DBDPE) to the SVHC Candidate List on November 5, 2025, classified as a PBT/vPvB substance. DBDPE is a high-volume brominated flame retardant widely used in electronics, wire insulation, and plastic housings as a replacement for legacy flame retardants. Its SVHC listing obligates suppliers of EU articles containing DBDPE above 0.1% w/w to communicate this to customers and, for importers, to notify ECHA.
2026-05-05 (6-month ECHA notification deadline for EU importers of articles, from Nov 2025 listing)
TSCA
8
updates
EPA Extends TCE Section 6(g) Exemption Provisions Postponement 90 Days
▼
Open TSCA Dashboard ↗
Monitor EPA Extends TCE Section 6(g) Exemption Provisions Postponement 90 Days HIGH 1.9
Federal Register · 2026-02-18
EPA extended the postponement of effectiveness for certain provisions of the final TCE TSCA Section 6 rule, specifically the conditions imposed on uses with Section 6(g) exemptions, by an additional 90 days from February 18, 2026. This continues to delay enforcement of restrictions on TCE-exempted industrial uses. Downstream manufacturers relying on TCE in degreasing or coating processes gain temporary regulatory relief but face eventual restriction.
Approximately May 19, 2026 (90 days from Feb 18, 2026)
Monitor EPA Extends Compliance Dates for Perchloroethylene and Carbon Tetrachloride TSCA Rules HIGH 1.4
EPA
EPA has extended compliance deadlines for its TSCA Section 6 final rules on perchloroethylene (PCE) and carbon tetrachloride, providing additional time for regulated entities to come into compliance with use restrictions and workplace controls. These rules impose restrictions on industrial and commercial uses of both solvents, which are common in manufacturing cleaning, degreasing, and coating operations. The extension does not eliminate compliance obligations but shifts near-term enforcement timelines.
Exact extended deadlines to be confirmed via Federal Register publication
Indirect EPA Proposes Updated Formaldehyde Emission Standards for Composite Wood Products MEDIUM 3.3
↗ Formaldehyde emission standards precedent signals EPA's intent to regulate volatile chemical emissions from building materials; our wood components and composite materials may face similar scrutiny.
EPA
EPA proposed updates to the Formaldehyde Emission Standards for Composite Wood Products under TSCA Title VI, adding a new quality control test method. This affects manufacturers and importers of composite wood products used in furniture, cabinetry, and construction components. Companies sourcing composite wood panels or finished goods containing composite wood must verify supplier compliance with updated emission testing protocols.
Comment period deadline TBD — monitor Federal Register for proposed rule publication date
EPA Seeks Comment on Renewal of TSCA Chemical Data Reporting Information Collection MEDIUM 4.9
Federal Register · 2026-02-24
EPA published a proposed renewal of the Chemical Data Reporting (CDR) Information Collection Request under TSCA Section 8(a), currently approved through October 31, 2026. CDR requires manufacturers and importers of chemicals above threshold quantities to report production volume, use, and exposure data on a four-year cycle. The next CDR reporting cycle will require submissions reflecting 2024 calendar year data.
Current ICR approval expires October 31, 2026; next CDR submission cycle anticipated 2025-2026
Monitor EPA Seeks Comment on Renewal of TSCA CBI Claims Information Collection LOW 1.6
Federal Register · 2026-02-25
EPA proposed renewal of the Confidential Business Information (CBI) Claims Information Collection Request under TSCA, currently approved through August 31, 2026. This ICR governs the administrative process for asserting and substantiating CBI claims in TSCA submissions, including PMNs and Section 8 reports. No substantive change to CBI claim requirements is proposed at this time.
Current ICR approval expires August 31, 2026
Monitor EPA Publishes New Chemical Findings for January 2026 PMNs and SNUNs LOW 1.0
Federal Register · 2026-03-23
EPA published Statements of Findings for new chemical submissions reviewed in January 2026, covering PMNs, MCANs, and SNUNs determined not likely to present unreasonable risk. These findings do not impose new restrictions but confirm EPA clearance for the listed substances to proceed to commercialization. Relevant only if the company or its suppliers submitted new chemical notices for substances in this batch.
Monitor EPA Publishes New Chemical Receipt and Status Data for September–December 2025 LOW 1.0
Federal Register · 2026-03-10
EPA published receipt and status information for new chemical submissions received between September and December 2025, including PMNs, SNUNs, MCANs, TME applications, and notices of commencement of manufacture. This is a routine transparency publication under TSCA Section 5 and does not impose new requirements. Useful for tracking whether any new fluoropolymer or coating-related chemistries have entered the TSCA review pipeline.
Monitor EPA Publishes New Chemical Findings for November–December 2025 Submissions LOW 1.0
Federal Register · 2026-03-04
EPA published Statements of Findings for PMNs, MCANs, and SNUNs reviewed in November–December 2025, confirming those substances are not likely to present unreasonable risk and may proceed to commercial use. No new restrictions are imposed by these findings. This batch should be cross-referenced against any alternative chemistry candidates being evaluated as part of PFAS reformulation efforts.
Prop65 2 new
6
updates
Prop 65 60-Day Notice Filed for Lead in Snack Bar via Sierra Trading Post
▼
Open Prop65 Dashboard ↗
New Monitor Prop 65 60-Day Notice Filed for Lead in Snack Bar via Sierra Trading Post LOW 1.0
CA AG — Prop 65 60-Day Notices · 2026-03-25
Notice 2026-01350 filed March 25, 2026 targets Nutrient Foods and Sierra Trading Post for lead in a food product sold through retail. Sierra Trading Post is a general merchandise retailer that also sells apparel, accessories, and gear — categories that overlap with industrial and consumer goods distribution channels.
Settlement or corrective action expected within 60 days of March 25, 2026 (by ~May 24, 2026)
New Monitor Clean Product Advocates Files Lead Notice Against Food Manufacturer and Sprouts LOW 1.0
CA AG — Prop 65 60-Day Notices · 2026-03-25
Notice 2026-01349 filed March 24, 2026 alleges lead violations in Abbot's Protein Veggie Burger sold at Sprouts Farmers Market. This continues a pattern of plaintiff law firm Clean Product Advocates targeting both brand manufacturers and their retail distributors simultaneously.
~May 23, 2026 (60 days from filing)
Indirect DEHP Phthalate Notice Filed Against Best Buy for Phone Cases MEDIUM 1.8
↗ DEHP enforcement in vinyl consumer products signals increasing Prop 65 litigation risk in flexible vinyl materials we do not directly use, but demonstrates plaintiff strategy expansion into hardware/electronics retail channels.
CA AG — Prop 65 60-Day Notices · 2026-03-25
Notice 2026-01348 filed March 24, 2026 names Best Buy and its multiple corporate entities as alleged violators for DEHP in Dapper Styles 5 Pack Cases — a vinyl/plastic consumer product sold through electronics retail. This is directly relevant: electronics retailers and distributors are being targeted for phthalates in plastic accessories, which mirrors the risk profile of vinyl components and protective accessories in the company's own supply chain.
~May 23, 2026 (60 days from filing)
Indirect DINP Phthalate Notice Filed Against EzPacking and Walmart for Toiletry Bags MEDIUM 2.0
↗ DINP phthalate enforcement in vinyl consumer products sets precedent for flexible vinyl regulation; we do not use DINP in windows/doors but this signals Prop 65 litigation momentum in vinyl materials that could expand to building product applications.
CA AG — Prop 65 60-Day Notices · 2026-03-25
Notice 2026-01340 filed March 16, 2026 alleges DINP violations in vinyl toiletry bags sold at Walmart, naming both the manufacturer and the retailer. DINP is the same phthalate of concern in the company's vinyl profile components, and the co-naming of the downstream retailer reinforces that distributors bear Prop 65 liability alongside manufacturers.
~May 15, 2026 (60 days from filing)
Indirect DEHP Notice Filed Against Evriholder and TJX for Vinyl Makeup Bags MEDIUM 1.8
↗ DEHP enforcement in flexible vinyl consumer products demonstrates Prop 65 plaintiff targeting of phthalate-containing vinyl goods; we do not use DEHP in windows/doors but trend signals potential future expansion to vinyl building materials.
CA AG — Prop 65 60-Day Notices · 2026-03-25
Notice 2026-01339 filed March 16, 2026 targets DEHP in a vinyl makeup organizer sold at HomeGoods and T.J. Maxx, with both manufacturer and retailer named. DEHP enforcement in flexible vinyl products is intensifying, directly relevant to any vinyl-profile or PVC-containing components the company sells into California.
~May 15, 2026 (60 days from filing)
Monitor Lead and Cadmium Notices Filed Against Seafood Manufacturers and Amazon LOW 1.0
CA AG — Prop 65 60-Day Notices · 2026-03-16
Multiple notices filed March 16, 2026 (2026-01341 through 2026-01344) target lead and cadmium in seafood products sold through Amazon, iHerb, and TJX — reinforcing that online marketplace operators are co-named in Prop 65 enforcement alongside upstream manufacturers. This pattern is relevant if the company sells any products through third-party marketplaces into California.
~May 15, 2026 (60 days from filing)
ConflictMinerals
3
updates
OSI Systems Files Form SD Conflict Minerals Report for Calendar Year 2025
▼
Open ConflictMinerals Dashboard ↗
Monitor OSI Systems Files Form SD Conflict Minerals Report for Calendar Year 2025 HIGH 1.9
SEC EDGAR — Form SD · 2026-03-23
OSI Systems Inc. (OSIS), a manufacturer of security and inspection systems with complex electronics supply chains, filed its annual Form SD conflict minerals disclosure with the SEC on March 23, 2026. The filing covers calendar year 2025 3TG sourcing and represents current industry practice for RCOI and due diligence reporting under Dodd-Frank Section 1502. As a peer manufacturer using sensors, control electronics, and automation components, OSI's disclosure benchmarks the level of supply chain inquiry expected by the SEC and by customer vendor compliance programs.
May 31, 2026 (standard Form SD annual filing deadline for calendar year 2025)
Monitor Alpha & Omega Semiconductor Files Form SD Conflict Minerals Disclosure HIGH 1.9
SEC EDGAR — Form SD · 2026-02-27
Alpha & Omega Semiconductor Ltd. (AOSL), a semiconductor manufacturer, filed its Form SD conflict minerals report on February 27, 2026, covering calendar year 2025 3TG use in its integrated circuits. Semiconductor manufacturers are upstream suppliers of tantalum capacitors and gold-contact components directly purchased by the company for automation electronics. This filing confirms that upstream semiconductor suppliers are actively completing RCOI and OECD-aligned due diligence, setting expectations for CMRT data quality the company should demand from its own direct suppliers.
May 31, 2026 (standard Form SD annual filing deadline for calendar year 2025)
Monitor NOAA Receives Deep Seabed Mining Exploration License Applications from Two Companies LOW 1.2
Federal Register · 2026-03-23
NOAA has accepted two applications under the Deep Seabed Hard Mineral Resources Act for seabed mining exploration licenses covering polymetallic nodule fields that contain cobalt, nickel, copper, and manganese — but not directly the 3TG minerals subject to Dodd-Frank Section 1502. Virtual public hearings and a comment period are underway. This development is low-relevance to near-term conflict minerals compliance obligations but signals a long-range shift in mineral supply geography that could eventually affect RCOI country-of-origin narratives for tantalum and tungsten sourcing alternatives.
ForcedLabor
4
updates
USTR Initiates Section 301 Investigations Into Forced Labor Import Failures
▼
Open ForcedLabor Dashboard ↗
Indirect USTR Initiates Section 301 Investigations Into Forced Labor Import Failures HIGH 3.5
↗ Section 301 investigations signal escalating US trade enforcement on forced labor; if applied to our supply chain materials (aluminum, vinyl, electronics, hardware from Asia), could trigger import delays or tariffs.
Federal Register · 2026-03-17
USTR launched Section 301 investigations on March 17, 2026 targeting economies that fail to prohibit importation of goods produced with forced labor. The investigations open a public comment period and will include hearings, signaling potential new tariffs or trade restrictions on goods from implicated countries. This is a significant escalation that could compound existing UFLPA enforcement, particularly for China-sourced components.
Public comment period open — monitor Federal Register for comment deadline
UFLPA Entity List Remains Active; CBP Enforcement Continues for Xinjiang-Linked Goods HIGH 5.0
DHS — UFLPA Entity List · 2026-03-25
DHS continues maintaining and updating the UFLPA Entity List, with CBP enforcing the rebuttable presumption against all goods with Xinjiang nexus. Importers must present clear and convincing evidence to overcome detention, including full supply chain traceability documentation. Electronics, aluminum, glass, and cotton-derivative components remain highest-risk categories for detention.
Ongoing — no fixed expiration; enforcement continuous
CBP Operational Guidance for Importers Governs UFLPA Detention Response Protocol HIGH 4.8
CBP — Forced Labor · 2026-03-25
CBP's operational guidance establishes the documentation standard importers must meet to rebut the UFLPA presumption and secure release of detained shipments. Required documentation includes complete supply chain mapping, proof of origin at each production stage, and evidence of no Xinjiang labor involvement. Failure to provide this documentation results in exclusion of the goods.
Ongoing enforcement; documentation must be ready prior to shipment arrival
Indirect Forced Labor Enforcement Task Force Maintains Strategic Oversight of UFLPA Implementation MEDIUM 3.7
↗ Task Force coordination on Entity List and sector-specific enforcement priorities may expand focus to building materials/hardware suppliers; warrants ongoing supply chain due diligence monitoring.
DHS — UFLPA · 2026-03-25
The DHS-led Forced Labor Enforcement Task Force continues to coordinate UFLPA strategy, including Entity List additions and sector-specific enforcement priorities. The Task Force publishes an annual strategy update that shapes which industries and supply chains face heightened scrutiny. Electronics and solar supply chains remain under active review.
Annual strategy updates — monitor for 2026 edition

REACH — ECHA has maintained steady Candidate List expansion activity over the past 6 months, adding three new SVHCs spanning flame retardants, industrial solvents, and fluorinated compound…
TSCA — The dominant theme over the past six months is a Trump EPA pulling back on enforcement timelines and rolling back or delaying Obama/Biden-era TSCA Section 6 risk management rules —…
Prop65 — All six articles in the monitoring window are concentrated in a single week of March 2026, indicating a sudden burst of 60-Day Notice activity rather than a sustained trend — the d…
ConflictMinerals — The 6-month regulatory signal for SEC Dodd-Frank conflict minerals is essentially flat in terms of new rulemaking — no SEC amendments, no new guidance, and no enforcement actions a…
ForcedLabor — UFLPA enforcement remains on an elevated, active trajectory with DHS and CBP maintaining consistent operational pressure on importers across all high-risk commodity categories. The…
